topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped brick. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. "Party cancelled," the subject line read. A slow, burning heat started in my chest, spreading outward like wildfire. I reread the message three times, searching for a typo, a mistake, anything to rationalize this utter absurdity. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white against the desk. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the otherwise silent apartment. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, the floorboards protesting under my sudden, agitated movements. Every instinct screamed at me to call, to demand an explanation, but a cold, heavy wave washed over me, leaving me stranded in a vortex of simmering fury.

[story 2]

I found out when I was scrolling through social media—a casual swipe, and then the stark, flat text confirming the event was off. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary gasp escaping my lips. For a full minute, nothing happened. Then, the air seemed to thin around me. My shoulders began to tense, pulling my neck into an uncomfortable rigidity. I stood by the window, staring out at the mundane street, but I couldn't see anything. My vision narrowed, focusing only on the injustice of it all. I walked back to the computer, my steps heavy and deliberate. I didn't type; I just stared at the screen until my eyes burned, a low, guttural sound building in my throat, vibrating with sheer disbelief and indignation.

[story 3]

When Sarah finally delivered the news, the words were delivered with a strained, overly bright smile that didn't reach her eyes. I absorbed the information like swallowing shards of glass. A deep, visceral churning started in my stomach, a physical manifestation of the sudden, violent shift within me. I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I let the silence stretch, letting the weight of the cancellation settle, heavy and suffocating. When I finally did respond, my voice was dangerously level, stripped bare of warmth. "Cancelled? You mean to tell me," I managed, the words clipped and sharp, "that all the planning, the effort, the anticipation—it was all for nothing?" I turned on my heel, walking towards the door with a purposeful, aggressive stride, needing to move, needing to physically escape the suffocating atmosphere of betrayal.
